rationale.R1.1: Paper makes an explicit first-in-field contribution claim ('first work to apply learned per-query routing to climate emulation'); evaluating whether the thesis and gap statement hold is plausibly central.
rationale.R1.2: The non-stationarity argument (§ on distribution shift) is a load-bearing logical chain: the premise that synoptic-scale inputs propagate distribution shift to the router is asserted but not derived — logical scrutiny is plausibly central.
rationale.R1.3: Specific quantitative claims are present (63% compute reduction, 4% CRPS penalty, 12M-parameter router, 8-year training window, 2018–2022 test split); checking whether stated numbers are internally consistent and adequately supported is plausibly central.
rationale.R1.4: The paper explicitly stakes a novelty claim; assessing the delta against nearest prior hybrid/emulator routing work is plausibly central.
rationale.R1.6: Peripherally relevant — a general 'what do you think?' might elicit stylistic observations, but writing is not the obvious primary ask.
rationale.R1.7: Peripherally relevant — citation coverage matters for validating the novelty claim, but the delegation does not foreground scholarship.

parsed.clarification_question: Is your primary interest a scientific/technical critique (evaluating the logical soundness of the non-stationarity argument, the quantitative claims, and the novelty assertion), or writing and presentation feedback (clarity, structure, prose quality)?
